    Large-Offset Supernovae

    Large-Offset Supernovae

    Type:Research-level concept
    Definition:SNe found at unusually large projected distances (>10 kpc) from the nearest galaxy nucleus
    Related: Hostless supernovae
    Type Ia supernovae
    Dynamical stellar displacement
    Galaxy mergers

    Large-offset supernovae are transient events found at unusually large projected distances from the nearest identified galaxy nucleus, typically >10 kpc. While some fraction arise from Type Ia progenitors in extended stellar halos or intracluster populations[1][2], others reside in tidal structures produced by galaxy mergers[3][4].

    Formation Channels

    Several mechanisms can produce large-offset SNe:

    • Tidal stripping: Stars redistributed into tidal tails and bridges during galaxy interactions can host SNe at tens of kpc from the host nucleus[3][4].
    • Collisional ring displacement: Old stellar populations swept outward by density waves in CRGs can produce Type Ia SNe within the expanding ring. SN 2025adpq at 11.4 kpc from G1 is a clear example[5].
    • Intracluster stellar populations: In galaxy clusters, tidally stripped stars form a diffuse intracluster component that can host SNe at >100 kpc from any cluster galaxy[1][2].
    • Progenitor redistribution: Galaxy interactions can redistribute old SN Ia progenitor systems to large radii, allowing explosions far from the progenitor birthplace[6][5].

    Offset Ranges

    Collisional rings can plausibly explain offsets of tens of kpc (ring radii typically 5–15 kpc)[5]. More extreme offsets (>100 kpc), such as the case of SN 2021hem, are unlikely to be explained by CRG displacement alone and likely require intracluster or hypervelocity progenitor explanations[5].

    Why This Matters

    Large-offset SNe challenge the standard assumption that a supernova's environment reflects its progenitor formation site. For Type Ia cosmology, host-galaxy-dependent corrections depend on accurate host identification. When SNe reside in faint tidal structures or collisional rings, the true host may be misidentified or missed entirely, introducing systematic biases[5].

    See Also

    • Hostless supernovae
    • Type Ia supernovae
    • Dynamical stellar displacement
    • Collisional ring galaxies
    • O'Connor et al. (2026) — SN 2025adpq

    References
    1. Gal-Yam, A. et al. (2003). "Hostless Type Ia SNe and intracluster stars." AJ, 125, 1087.
    2. Sand, D. J. et al. (2011). "Large-offset Type Ia SNe in clusters."
    3. Schweizer, F. et al. (2008). "Type Ia SNe in tidal structures." AJ, 136, 1482.
    4. Ferretti, R. et al. (2017). "Large-offset Type Ia SNe in tidal features."
    5. O'Connor, B. et al. (2026). "SN 2025adpq." arXiv:2603.15899
    6. Ditrani, E. et al. (2024). "Redistribution of old progenitors by galaxy interactions."
